description: To grieve or mourn: the sustained, living form of sadness that follows loss. Built visibly on 'nuhe' (sadness) plus the inward 'mi': grieving as carrying sadness within oneself, over time, as a practice rather than a single feeling. The derivation teaches itself — anyone who knows 'nuhe' can read 'nuhemi'.
sound_symbolism: It begins as 'nuhe' begins — the grounded nasal sinking into the deepest vowel, the exhale through 'he' — but where sadness breathes out and ends, grief turns inward: the final 'mi' is the self, holding. Sadness carried, not dropped. The word is one syllable longer than 'nuhe' because grief lasts longer than sadness.
grammatical_notes: Verb: 'mia nuhemi' (I grieve / I am mourning). With the object grieved for marked by 'wei': 'mia wei ni melu nuhemi' (I grieve for a dear friend). Companion words: 'nuhe' (sadness), 'lumeo' (die/death), 'phamo' (console), 'therilu' (rest).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Grief is attachment meeting impermanence — not a flaw to fix but a truth to sit with. 'Nuhemi' names the sitting-with, giving mourning the dignity of its own word.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Communities that can speak grief can share it. A dedicated word invites condolence, ritual, and accompaniment instead of silence.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Mourning was structured time — worn, sung, and accompanied. The word honors grief as a season, not an inconvenience.

semantic_domains.emotion: The sustained carrying of sadness after loss
semantic_domains.ritual: The practice and season of mourning
